data.title: Cedar Creek Partners Q1 2025 Results
data.description:
Dear Partners and Friends:

Most of the major indices were hit pretty hard in the first quarter as investors retreated from the major tech stocks. In the first quarter, the NASDAQ declined 10.4% and the S&P500 (SPY) fell 4.3% largely due to the major tech stocks that hurt the NASDAQ. Other indices we track declined comparably. Cedar Creek was up 8.7% on the quarter, net of fees and expenses.[1]

While Cedar Creek Partners focuses primarily on microcap stocks, and over-the-counter stocks in particular, we compare our returns against larger indices as well since we believe we need to outperform the most prominent passive benchmarks over time in order to justify our existence.

Cedar Creek’s average annual return over our 19-year history is 14.3%, net of fees and expenses, which compares favorably to all the indices we compare against. Cumulative returns since inception for Cedar Creek were 1,204.5%, net of fees and expenses.

$100,000 invested in the fund at inception in January 2006 would have grown to $1,304,476 as of March 31, 2025, net of fees and expenses, whereas $100,000 invested in the indices we compare against would have only grown to between $272,159 in the Russell MicroCap and $746,612 in the NASDAQ.

Fund Holdings are at Incredibly Attractive Prices

On the whole, as of the end of March 2025, the fund’s holdings were trading at 8.0 times our estimate of earnings for the coming year, and 6.6 times expected earnings net of cash at the respective businesses.[2] Trailing earnings multiple was 9.8 times.

Weighted price-to-book was 1.5. Dividend yield was 3.4%. Weighted expected return on equity as of March 31, 2025, was 18.5%.

Cash Levels and Fund Repositioning

We started the quarter with cash levels at 8% and ended the quarter at 7% largely due to the addition of one meaningful investment along with additions to recent investment. There were no major sales in the quarter.

We continued to increase our position in Western Capital Resources (OTC:WCRS) which we discussed in our 2024 year-end letter. Just before the end of the quarter, Western Capital announced a tender offer for 666,667 shares or roughly 11% of the company at $15 per share, or a total price of $10 million. The tender price was at a meaningful premium to previous trades. Included in the tender documents were 2024 results. 2024 net income was $6.7 million or $1.12 per share based on year-end share count. If we add back intangible amortization, cash earnings were about $9.1 million, or $1.52 per share based on year-end share count. Net cash was nearly $6 per share. The share price rose in the quarter from $9.00 to $15.00 per share. Earnings for 2024 were negatively impacted by their acquisition of Northern Brewer which accounting rules required them to consolidate for the full year. WCRS purchased the business and appears to have relocated it to one of their existing operations to reduce facility and potentially fulfillment costs.

In addition to WCRS, we added to Evercel (OTC:EVRC) another position we mentioned in our last letter. We spent more time on the issue of whether the shares continue to trade with the rights to its minority investment in ZAGG, the maker of cell phone covers and screen protectors, which Evercel contributed to a liquidating trust. We are more convinced that the shares do trade with the rights to proceeds and that the company’s position is incorrect. We expect a binary outcome. We are either wrong and the value will be near zero or we are right and the value will be based on the proceeds from the eventual sale of ZAGG.

Shares of Evercel are currently at $0.30 per share just a bit above our average cost basis. Evercel has 27.1 million shares for a market cap of just $7 million. ZAGG was purchased in 2021 for $200 million, $70 million of which was equity. Evercel paid $36 million for just over 50% equity ownership, which was later reduced to 40%. ZAGG was expected to generate about $500 million in sales. If ZAGG was still worth what Evercel paid in 2021 it would be worth $28 million or just over $1.00 per Evercel share, or four times the price we paid for shares. If sales have grown, and/or debt has been reduced, ZAGG’s value could be higher, which would mean Evercel’s value would be even higher.

Expert Market Exposure

Our exposure to stocks trading in the expert market increased to 35% of the fund. Expert market stocks are companies impacted by SEC Rule 15c2-11. For those unfamiliar, the rule prevents brokers from not only displaying quotes for non-reporting companies but also restricts transactions to selling only. Institutional accounts, depending on the broker, are not subject to the buying restriction.

There are a wide range of differences among “non-reporting” companies. Some are completely dark – meaning they do not communicate any financial results to shareholders. Others update quarterly or annually on their website, sometimes behind a password login. Others mail annual results out to shareholders, or just shareholders of record, but do not post them publicly.

Four positions make up about 80% of the fund’s expert market exposure – Propel Media (OTC:PROM) is about 12% of the fund, First IC (OTC:FIEB) is 9%, PD-Rx Pharmaceuticals (OTC:PDRX) and Pacific Coast Oil Trust (OTC:ROYTL) are each roughly 3% of the fund.

Most of the fund’s outperformance in the first quarter was due to the increase in the valuation of our expert market holdings. Our expert market basket of stocks increased in value by 35% in the first quarter, while our “control” positions suffered a modest decline, and our generally undervalued securities increased modestly.

At the beginning of the year, Propel, which does not report financial results to shareholders, was valued at $0.603 per share, based on the fund manager’s decision to use a 6% dividend discount model. At quarter end, it was valued at $0.85 per share. That valuation matched the last sales price, as well as management’s 6% dividend discount model based on their April 2 dividend announcement. The most recent bid price was $0.85 per share while ask was $1.00 per share.

PD-Rx was valued at last sale of $3.05 per share at the beginning of the year and $2.62 per share at the end of the quarter. Pacific Coast Oil Trust was valued at last sale, which was $0.26 per unit at year-end and at the end of the first quarter.

Fund performance in the quarter also benefitted from MetroCity Bankshares (MCBS) announcing a merger with First IC. Both are Korean focused banks headquartered in Doraville, Georgia. Under the agreement, First IC shareholders will receive approximately 0.3734 shares of MetroCity and cash of approximately $12.35 per share, or an approximate total value of $22.58 per share. The value First IC shareholders will receive will fluctuate based on changes in MetroCity’s share price. The merger is expected to close in the fourth quarter of 2025. First IC was valued at last trade to start the year, which was $9.30 per share. We received a $1 per share dividend in the quarter. At quarter end it was valued at a 20% discount to the expected proceeds, or $20.00 per share. 3 Last trade was $17.25 per share. The current bid as of quarter end was $18.45 per share and ask was $20.55 per share. Half of the fund’s gains in the quarter were due to the increase in First IC.

We continue to allocate a meaningful portion of the fund’s portfolio to expert market stocks based on the belief that they will generate satisfactory returns for the fund over time.

Update on Propel Media

Propel Media – has not publicly reported financials since 2019. We are completely left on our own to figure out revenue and profitability. We do know that there are 252 million shares outstanding. We also know that in 2022 they had agreed to be acquired by IQVIA Holdings (IQV) for between $700 and $800 million ($2.75 to $3.15 per share); however, in early 2024 the deal was blocked by the FTC and both Propel and IQVIA decided to walk away rather than fight. In June 2023, prior to the deal being blocked, we were able to purchase shares for $0.24 per share after becoming aware of the deal in published reports.

We purchased the stock in hopes of the deal going through. If it did, we expected a ridiculous 10-12x return. If it didn’t, we believed we were still likely getting a bargain. We doubted that the next best liquidity event would value the company at 1/10 of the potential merger price with IQVIA. We still believe that and are willing to wait.

Unexpectedly, Propel began paying a quarterly dividend in August 2023. In February of this year we received a dividend of just over $0.02 per share, nearly double the dividend of the year ago quarter and the previous quarter. In total we have received nearly $0.076 per share in dividends, or 33% of our purchase price in just over 20 months, and will receive another $0.01 per share in April (see chart below).[4]

The April 2025 dividend is very interesting. The per-share amount is twice the May 2024 dividend ($0.01 versus $0.005) yet covers only 52 days versus 94 days. It seems reasonable to assume the improvement in the business as reflected in the increased dividend in February is continuing. Why Propel shortened the timeframe between dividends is a mystery to us.

Another interesting data point we track is Propel’s level of borrowing and its loan capacity. Propel’s main business, DeepIntent, has an asset-based loan (ABL) facility with a publicly reporting company, SLR Investment Corp (SLRC). SLR lists all their loans each quarter and also their unfunded commitments. This allows us to track the level of debt owed by DeepIntent but also gives us a good idea of their current revenue run rate. It is our understanding that most lenders limit borrowing capacity to 85 to 90% of a company’s accounts receivable. It seems reasonable to assume DeepIntent grants 60 to 75 day payment terms on its accounts receivables. Based on this we can estimate DeepIntent’s revenue run rate. It would be the current ABL loan amount (borrowed column below) divided by 85 to 90% to give us total receivables, and then multiplied by six (to annualize the sixty days of receivables to 365 days) to give us approximate annualized revenue run rate (ranges shown in four columns of chart below).

What we learn is that annualized revenue was likely between $90 and $120 million at the end of 2022 (first line of chart). That revenue appears to have risen to between $112 and $150 million as of the end of 2023, an increase of nearly 25%. By the end of 2024 the revenue run rate appears to have been between $200 and $266 million, or an increase of nearly 80% from a year earlier (last line of the chart). This would be consistent with the increased dividend (the trailing twelve-month dividend has risen 45% in the last year). To say we are eagerly waiting to read SLR’s first quarter filing is an understatement.

Update on Our Control Positions

By control, we mean that the fund manager is on the Board or in a position to significantly influence decision-making, not that he has total control and gets what he wants. Both Solitron and PharmChem have strong independent boards, which is the way it should be at all companies.

Solitron Devices (OTCPK:SODI) - the bid price for shares decreased from $15.85 per share to $14.28 per share during the first quarter. As a reminder, the fund manager is CEO and a board member of Solitron. The fund owns 11.6% of Solitron’s outstanding shares and the fund manager owns 2.5% personally. We announced fiscal third quarter results in January. Earnings were $266,000, or $0.13 per share.

In the fiscal third quarter, Solitron had its largest quarterly bookings (orders) in at least twenty years. Bookings were $8.0 million in the quarter, versus sales of $3.4 million. Bookings included a large two-year order for HIMARS (High Mobility Artillery Rocket System) components from L3Harris – Solitron’s second-largest defense customer. In the fiscal fourth quarter, Solitron did even better. Bookings in the quarter were $8.9 million versus $2.2 million the prior year fiscal fourth quarter. Total bookings for the fiscal year were $20.8 million versus $12.8 million in the prior fiscal year. Sales in the fiscal fourth quarter were $3.1 million versus $4.0 million in the prior year quarter. For the 2025 fiscal year sales were $13.9 million versus $12.8 million in fiscal 2024. Bookings in the fiscal fourth quarter included over $5 million from Solitron’s largest defense customer, RTX (formerly Raytheon) for AMRAAM (Advanced Medium-Range Air-to-Air Missile) components. The order is believed to include parts for two or three years of SM-6 missile production as well, which is reportedly used, or intended to be used, to shoot down hypersonic missiles.

Backlog at Solitron was $18.1 million as of the end of the fiscal 2025 year (February 28, 2025) versus $11.2 million at the end of the 2024 fiscal year (February 29, 2024). Please see our 2024 year-end letter, for an overview of Solitron’s annual meeting held in January.

PharmChem (OTCPK:PCHM) – the fund is the largest owner, at 33% of outstanding shares, and I serve as Chairman of the Board. The bid price for shares increased from $3.07 per share to $3.20 in the first quarter of 2025. PharmChem makes sweat patches used to detect drugs of abuse. It is an alternative to urine testing. Fourth quarter earnings were $0.065 per share versus $0.066 per share in the prior year quarter, although it should be noted that the prior year had minimal income taxes unlike the current year. Pre-tax income increased from $319,000 in the 2023 fourth quarter to $402,000 in the 2024 fourth quarter. The company is debt free and had $1.6 million in cash, or $0.35 per share.

In January 2025, we announced the exploration of strategic alternatives (link). The release noted that PharmChem will consider various options, including acquisitions, potential sale of the Company, merger or a debt financed special dividend. PharmChem has no meaningful tariff exposure.

Update on Other Fund Holdings

Citizens Bancshares (OTCPK:CZBS) - is an Atlanta, Georgia based bank that received $95.7 million of additional capital via the US Treasury’s Emergency Capital Investment Program (ECIP) at the end of June 2022. The bid price decreased from $51.15 per share at the beginning of the year to $51.00 per share at the end of the first quarter. During the quarter, they paid a $1.10 per share dividend. Citizens has reduced its share count by 10% over the last two years, from 2.0 million shares to 1.8 million shares. Earnings for the first nine months of 2024 were $4.55 per share versus $4.23 per share in the first nine months of 2023. Results in both years were negatively impacted by $3 million loan loss provisions on the same loan that has now been fully reserved. Citizens has not officially released 2024 results although call reports show Q4 2024 results were in line with the prior year.

Due to the ECIP funds Citizens is one of the most over capitalized banks in the US. Annualized earnings run rate after preferred stock expense is roughly $7.50 per share. The stock is an incredible bargain at seven times earnings for a massively overcapitalized bank that is shareholder friendly. We expect them to either make an acquisition or increase organic growth, all the while continuing to return capital to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases.

ENDI Corp (OTCQB:ENDI) – we profiled ENDI in our 2024 first quarter letter. The share price rose during the first quarter from $11.43 per share to $12.00 per share. ENDI owns CrossingBridge Advisors which manages fixed income mutual funds and a few managed accounts. Assets under management (AUM) for CrossingBridge grew over 30% in 2024, from $2.6 billion at the beginning of 2024, adjusted for the acquisition of the RiverPark Short Term High Yield Fund, to $3.4 billion at the end of 2024. According to their website, as of the end of March 2025, AUM was $3.8 billion, an increase of 10% in the first quarter alone. We think the current interest rate environment is attractive for short-term bonds.

ENDI’s third and fourth quarter operating margins were 32% but that is misleading. ENDI incurs significant amortization charges related to its acquisition of CrossingBridge and other investment management contracts. Adjusted operating margin was 47 to 48%. Fourth quarter cash earnings were $0.35 per share, or $1.40 per share annualized.[5] Net cash and investments at the end of 2024 were approximately $2.50 per share, meaning the stock was trading at just seven times earnings, net of cash, at quarter end.

Our fair value estimate just keeps rising as the company continues to execute. We wrote in our 2024 year-end letter that, based on their recent growth, a valuation of 12 to 15 times cash earnings plus net cash seems more reasonable. Using 12 to 15 times the current run rate of cash earnings plus net cash would result in a value between $19.30 and $23.50 per share.[6]

Subsequent to quarter end, ENDI announced a cash investment in CrossingBridge in exchange for 25% membership interest. The price was $25.9 million. The fund, and other entities affiliated with the fund manager, participated in the transaction. We think it is a win-win deal. It shows the value of CrossingBridge and provides additional capital to good capital allocators to grow assets under management.

New Position in the Fund

During the quarter we built up a new position in the fund, Skyline Bankshares (OTCQX:SLBK), a community bank in rural Virgina and Tennessee. The bank has 5,557,000 shares outstanding at roughly $12.50 per share for a market cap of $70 million. The bank earned $1.34 per share in 2024, down from $1.74 in 2023. At first glance, things appear to be moving in the wrong direction. That is a big decrease in earnings. In 2024, Skyline acquired a bank in Tennessee. That resulted in merger related expenses, and amortization of core deposit intangibles.

In 2023, Skyline earned $12 million before taxes, and $9.7 million after tax. In 2024, it earned $9.3 million before tax and $7.4 million after tax. Skyline incurred $2.5 million in merger related expenses and increased core deposit intangibles. Absent those expenses, earnings would have been nearly flat. If we look just at the fourth quarter of 2024, the first full quarter after the acquisition, adjusted pre-tax earnings would have been $4.26 million or $3.41 million after tax, which would be $0.61 per share. Annualized that would come to $2.45 per share. We also think additional cost savings may be possible that did not show up in the first full quarter since the merger. We were able to purchase shares at $12.50 per share, or just over five times earnings. Current dividend yield is a solid 4%. We believe that Skyline should trade closer to ten times earnings, or $25 per share.

Change in Fund Administrator

The fund was recently notified by Unkar that they planned to close operations as of the end of 2024. We have changed our administrator to Fleming Fund Services as of January 2025. If anyone is not receiving their monthly statements, please let us know.

Tax Information and K-1's

Investor K-1’s were issued electronically on April 1. The fund’s annual audit report was sent out on April 15. If any of the fund’s investors did not receive either, please contact us.

Room for New Members and/or Additional Funds

We continue to have more attractive ideas than capital. Thus, there is plenty of room for existing partners to increase their investment and for others to join. Please consider referring friends of yours who may be potential new investors. The basic requirements are 1) that each invests a minimum of $100,000 and 2) that new members are accredited (high net worth) individuals. Subsequent investments must be for a minimum of $10,000.

If this letter was passed on to you, and you would like to be added to our monthly distribution list, please email me at the email address below. You can find more letters at eriksencapitalmgmt.com/investor-letters. Should you have any questions regarding the fund, please don’t hesitate to call or email.

Sincerely,

Tim Eriksen, Manager, Cedar Creek Partners LLC

Footnotes

[1] While, no single index is directly comparable to Cedar Creek Partners, we believe that it is important to compare our performance to a passively managed approach. At the core of our investment philosophy is the belief that we can generate superior risk-adjusted returns by holding a more concentrated portfolio of under-valued securities, than an index holding a far greater number of securities. Index returns are calculated from information reported on Yahoo! Finance, Dow Jones, and Russell (see DISCLAIMER for more information).

[2] Ratio excludes cash held by the fund. We add back non-economic amortization in our earnings estimate. Due to the uncertainty of the situation, we did not attribute any earnings or book value for Pacific Coast Oil Trust (OTC:ROYTL). We did not include excess capital of our ECIP bank holdings as excess cash.

[3] ($22.58 expected proceeds divided by 20% discount to the power of 0.67 to capture the time element remaining until expected close, or $22.58/((1+20%)^0.67). We chose this model due to the lack of liquidity in the shares preventing typical arbitrage from taking place.

[4] We believe the dividends are due to net income since they are reported as dividends and not return of capital.

[5] Accounting Note: We ignore the impact of warrant liability change on reported earnings. While we understand the intent, it is one of our least favorite accounting rules. If the stock price goes up during the quarter the company is hit with a non-cash expense since the “cost” of the previously issued warrants is now higher. Conversely, if the stock declines in the quarter the company reports a credit to expense. The actual pricing of the warrants remains unchanged under both scenarios. The end result is the better the business does the worse reported results are and vice versa. That makes no sense to us. We prefer the impact be captured in diluted earnings per share.

[6] A slight discount might be needed to account for outstanding warrants.

Disclaimers

Fund Performance

The financial performance figures for 2025 presented in this report are un-audited estimates based on the best information available at the time of the letter and are subject to subsequent revision by the Fund’s auditors. Past performance may not be indicative of future results and no representation is made that an investor will or is likely to achieve results similar to those shown. All investments involve risk including the loss of principal.

Net Return reflects the experience of an investor who came into the Fund on inception and did not add to or withdraw from the Fund through the end of the most recently reported period. The reported net return figures will therefore include the impact of high water marks in the cumulative return. Individual investor returns will vary depending upon the timing of their investment, the effects of additions and withdrawals from their capital account, and each individual’s high water mark figure, if any.

Index Returns

The S&P500 Index returns are reported using the S&P500 Depository Receipt Trust (SPDR) which trades under the ticker symbol SPY. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the SPY performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

Nasdaq performance excludes dividends, which historically have been immaterial to the total return of that index. In recent years more technology stocks have begun paying dividends thus the inclusion of dividends would increase the reported figures.

Russell 2000 performance is from data reported on Russell’s website and includes reinvested dividends.

DJIA returns are reported using the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average which trades under the ticker symbol DIA. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the DIA performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

While reported returns for SPY and DIA will likely be a few tenths of a percentage lower than the representative index annually, we believe they are a better reflection of what a non-institutional investor would earn following a passive investment approach.

Index returns are provided as a convenience to the reader only. The Fund’s returns are likely to differ substantially from that of any index, and there can be no assurance that the Fund will achieve results that are superior to such indices.

Share Prices

Share price figures for listed stocks are from Yahoo! Finance and unless specified otherwise are the closing price as of the previous month end. Share price figures for unlisted stocks are closing bid prices as reported on otcmarkets.com, except for unlisted stocks classified as expert market, which do not have public availability of quotes, and are marked to last sale.

Forward-Looking Statements

This letter and the accompanying discussion include forward-looking statements. All statements that are not historical facts are forward-looking statements, including any statements that relate to future market conditions, results, operations, strategies or other future conditions or developments and any statements regarding objectives, opportunities, positioning or prospects. Forward-looking statements are necessarily based upon speculation, expectations, estimates and assumptions that are inherently unreliable and subject to significant business, economic and competitive uncertainties and contingencies. Forward-looking statements are not a promise or guaranty about future events.

data.title: Benitec Biopharma: An Under-The-Radar And Undervalued Gene Therapy Developer
data.description:
Introduction

We initiate a Buy rating for Benitec Biopharma (NASDAQ:BNTC). In our opinion, the early data from BB 301, a one‑time gene therapy for oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy (OPMD), are promising, and the company’s potential cash runway into mid-2027 offers a clear path to key readouts without immediate dilution.

We believe BB 301 could deliver meaningful, durable improvements in swallowing function for OPMD patients. Today, OPMD patients have no approved options.

We are confident Benitec has a promising commercial opportunity ahead of it. We also think the current price does not fully reflect the likelihood of positive data from the upcoming high‑dose cohort of BB 301 or the potential value of a one‑time therapy in a rare disease such as OPMD.

Stock Performance

BNTC shares trade near $12.58, up about 103% year to date. The stock’s 52‑week range is $4.75 to $16.90. The stock is largely under the radar in the gene therapy sector. After all, trading volume is very small for its stock, with only 54,547 shares traded on April 11, 2025.

We think the current price does not fully reflect the likelihood of positive data from the upcoming high‑dose cohort of BB 301 or the potential value of a one‑time therapy in a rare disease.

Pipeline Developments

The company's primary pipeline candidate is BB 301. In fact, when we reviewed the company's website, BB 301 was the only candidate listed in its pipeline. BB 301 uses a single viral vector to both silence the faulty PABPN1 gene and deliver a healthy copy. Early data in a small group of patients showed improved swallowing strength and no serious safety issues. We think the biggest challenge ahead will be ensuring consistent swallowing tests across sites.

The high‑dose cohort, expected to start dosing in 2H 2025, should clarify whether higher doses bring greater benefit without new risks. Based on prior dosing timelines for the low-dose cohort, which began in November 2023 and produced interim data by April 2024, we think it is possible that the high-dose cohort could complete dosing by late 2025 or early 2026. We think the study could reasonably conclude in 2026, which would facilitate a Phase 3 trial beginning later in the year.

Importantly, though, it appears that the company has not guided when the ongoing Phase 1b/2a study will conclude, and without this information, it is difficult to forecast when the study might end. This makes projecting the start of a Phase 3 trial purely speculative at this point, though we offer what, we believe, is a reasoned set of possible timelines below.

If the topline data are positive from the study, it could pave the way to a Phase 3 trial. In terms of timing from there, the FDA has estimated that a phase 3 trial can take anywhere between 1 and 4 years to complete, and market research indicates it took an average of 1.3 years from the end of the phase 3 trial to FDA approval.

Thus, in the best case, assuming the phase 3 trial starts in sometime in 2026, the trial could wrap up in 2027 and BB 301 could be approved in 2028. This seems to be consistent with Oppenheimer's latest forecast. In our view, however, a more likely scenario, is that, given the same assumption, the trial wraps up in 2028 and approval occurs in 2029.

And the worst case, in our view, is that the trial does not wrap up until 2029 or 2030, which would push FDA approval potentially out to 2030 or 2031. Again, this assumes a 2026 start for the trial, but this ultimately depends on when the current Phase 1/2a study is completed, and it appears that the company has not guided on that yet.

Development of BB 301 has been supported by a collaboration with Axovant Sciences in 2018. Under that deal, Benitec received $10 million upfront, is eligible for $17.5 million in near‑term milestones, up to $187.5 million in total milestones, and retains 30 percent of net profits on worldwide sales of AXO AAV OPMD.

Benitec has also collaborated with Biomics Biotechnologies on a hepatitis B program. Per the terms of the collaboration, "Benitec will pay Biomics AUD$2.5 million upfront with a further AUD$3.5 million upon successful commercialization of the program. In addition, Biomics will receive a single-digit royalty on net sales once the product is marketed." While this collaboration remains a very early stage, it could broaden Benitec's pipeline down the road. As such, it is too early to evaluate this program.

Market Opportunity

We believe the market opportunity for Benitec is strong. The Cleveland Clinic has estimated a 3,000 to 30,000 OPMD patient population in the United States, with a prevalence of about 1 in 100,000 in western countries. However, in 2023, the company estimated there were 15,000 OPMD patients in the United States, Western Europe, Canada, and Israel.

Moreover, our market research indicates that the global OPMD market is estimated at $5.11 billion in 2024 and is expected to hit $25.33 billion by 2037. It is unclear whether the company has ever released any of its internal projections about the market opportunity for BB 301 in the United States or globally.

To get a better understanding of the market opportunity, it is worth discussing what BB 301 might be priced at. Gene therapies for rare diseases tends to be very expensive. To list some, Luxturna costs $850,000 for both eyes, Zolgensma $2.1 million per patient, and Hemgenix $3.5 million per patient. Consistent with those examples, we think BB 301 could be priced anywhere between $1 million and $3 million for a one‑time treatment.

Thus, even with modest uptake, we think revenue could be substantial. After all, if just 300 to 500 people out of 10,000 receive BB 301 at $1 million each, annual sales would fall between $300 million and $500 million. Of course, final pricing, patient access, and reimbursement will determine the real outcome, and all this assumes clinical success going forward and regulatory approval. Needless to say, none of these milestones are guaranteed.

Financial Overview

In Q2 2025 (ended December 31, 2024) Benitec reported no revenue and operating expenses of $8.6 million, resulting in a net loss of $7.4 million. The company also reported cash and equivalents of $78.3 million.

With respect to cash flow from operations, the company lost $6.2 million in Q1 2024, $3.3 million in Q2, $4.6 million in Q3, and $7.7 million in Q4. With such a large increase in burn in Q4, we think it is reasonable to assume burn is on an upward trajectory in 2025.

The company priced a $30 million securities offering on March 25, 2025, which was expected to close the following day. We expect that this will be reflected in Q3 2025.

Assuming a quarterly burn of $7.7 million going forward, then the $78 million cash balance provides a cash runway into mid-2027. It is unclear whether the securities offering closed and whether $30 million was raised. Assuming it closed on time and $30 million was, in fact, raised, that could push the cash runway into early 2028.

That being said, it is reasonable to assume the burn rate will continue to significantly accelerate as BB 301 advances. So, the risk of dilution prior to early 2028, in our opinion, is high.

Risks

An investment in Benitec carries significant risks that are worthy of discussion in this bullish article. The primary risk, in our view, is that the high‑dose cohort uncovers safety concerns and/or the therapy is not efficacious in a broader patient group. We think any such clinical failure for BB 301 would be catastrophic for the company, as this appears to be the company's only shot on goal at the moment.

Additionally, even if the company is successful in the clinic, it still may not receive FDA approval. And even if it receives FDA approval, it does not automatically mean that the company will successfully commercialize BB 301.

Second to clinical and/or commercial failure risk is that additional capital raises may be required, which would dilute existing shareholders. As discussed in the Financial Overview section above, we think there is a high risk of dilution before 2028, as R&D expenses rise with the continued advancement of BB 301. The significant increase in cash burn in Q4 2024 underscores this risk. Dilution could come much earlier than we anticipate if the company introduces new pipeline candidates or experiences setbacks in clinic and/or other unexpected disruptions.

Conclusion

In our view, Benitec offers a compelling risk‑reward profile. We ultimately think BB 301 will be successful in the clinic and the company will successfully commercialize the therapy after it is approved in or around 2029. The upcoming high‑dose data in mid-2026 represents a significant catalyst, and we believe the stock is well positioned to continue its strong performance as these milestones are met. For those reasons, we initiate a Buy rating.

data.title: Unity Bancorp: Batting Away Tariff Concerns
data.description:
Given everything that's happening with tariffs, you'd be forgiven for thinking that banks were looking at a pretty dour outlook right now. Not necessarily, at least according to Unity Bancorp (NASDAQ:UNTY). This small New Jersey lender was among the first banks to release first-quarter results - and if management's forward-looking remarks hold true, talk about a downturn may be a tad premature. Oh, and for what it's worth, Unity's first-quarter numbers were excellent too.

As solid as things look at the business level, Unity's stock hasn't held up quite as well. It's true that it's only down around 10% this year - and that's pretty good compared to regional bank ETFs - but that's after being up around 17% in early February. The stock has more or less performed in line with peers since then, falling by 20% or so (including dividends).

Is that drop enough to make Unity a bargain? I think so. These shares now trade for just under 1.3x book value, down from around 1.7x a couple of months ago. Given Unity's strong return on equity and growth, that multiple is considerably cheaper than it may look on your screen.

Few Signs Of Trouble For Now

Unity released first quarter financials last week. The numbers themselves were pretty impressive. Net profit landed at $11.6 million, or $1.13 per share, which doesn't sound like much, except that this bank only has around $2.5 billion in assets and $305 million in stockholders' equity. The associated profitability metrics - a 1.83% annualized ROA and a 15.6% ROE - are excellent.

Like many banks, margin dynamics have been improving for Unity lately, aided by lower interest rates. Because the bank doesn't enjoy particularly cheap funding costs, it has reasonable scope to reduce its interest bill when rates are contracting. That is basically what is happening now. Expressed relative to its earning assets, Unity's cost of funding fell another 10bps sequentially last quarter to 2.18%.

At the same time, there are some positive trends on the asset side as well. While the Fed has lowered short-term rates, yields at the long-end of the curve have been rising. Commercial and residential mortgages account for the vast majority of Unity's lending, and these are often priced relative to long-term Treasury yields. That trend will have been a tailwind, with Unity's blended loan yield increasing around 4bps last quarter to 6.70%.

Related to that, Unity is still putting loans on the book at a solid clip, with period-average balances rising 3.3% sequentially last quarter to $2.3 billion. Loans represent over 90% of the bank's interest-earning assets. As a result, Unity's overall earning-asset yield has remained relatively buoyant, also expanding 4bps last quarter to 6.68%. Those dynamics were good for 9bps of sequential NIM expansion - which at 4.46% is one of the healthiest in the space.

Tariffs only really became a story around a week or so ago. Given that, you could certainly argue that first quarter financials are already redundant and that guidance is what the market will really be interested in. Asset quality and loan demand are two things I will be paying particular attention to. On the latter, management's commentary was actually pretty bullish:

Despite the recent volatility seen in the capital markets primarily due to the implementation of tariffs, as a community bank we do not see any adverse impacts on prospective loan demand. A portion of our small business customers are poised to benefit from tariffs on foreign goods.

James A. Hughes, President and CEO, Unity Bancorp, Inc.

As for asset quality, there were some signs of deterioration last quarter, with Unity's nonperforming loans ratio increasing 16bps sequentially to ~0.77%. That's not extraordinary relative to historical marks, but it'll be something to monitor going forward. Actual loan losses do remain low, however, with net charge-offs only clocking in at around 9bps.

More generally, I would keep in mind that loans are a hefty 90% of Unity's total assets, and that's a very high concentration compared to the average peer (somewhere in the 70% region). Accordingly, loan losses are going to be magnified here in terms of their impact on profitability. That is just a feature of this bank's makeup.

Per above, Unity mostly writes commercial and residential mortgages. These account for around 75% of the loan book. Loan-to-value ratios look modest enough in the commercial book (sub-60% on average), and the bank is also generating a circa 2.5% pre-tax, pre-provision ROA from which it can mop up credit charges. Unity is also more than adequately capitalized, with its CET1 Capital Ratio landing just shy of 13.9% at the end of last quarter. I think it would take something pretty nasty to really hurt this bank, but that's still a risk to consider.

The Valuation

Like I said in the introduction, Unity doesn't look like the cheapest bank stock out there, at least at first blush. It ended last quarter with $30.38 per share in book value, putting the current share price ($39.14) at just under 1.3x that number.

Place that in its correct context, though, and things look quite a bit more attractive. First of all, Unity earned a mid-teens ROE last quarter. That's also what it has been earning in recent years as well. Assuming that holds up, the stock's implied P/E ratio would be less than 9x. Only a small number of sell-side analysts cover the bank, but that's basically where they land as well, with FY2025 consensus EPS putting the shares at just over 8x.

Moreover, Unity is currently paying out less than 15% of its earnings via dividends. Sure, that means the yield is low - per Seeking Alpha it is just 1.4% - but its high level of retained earnings is supporting a lot of organic growth. The bank has roughly doubled its per-share book value over the past five years. Interest-earning asset growth has been slightly more pedestrian in that time, but still strong, growing by around 70% on a per-share basis.

All said, I think the stock offers investors a compelling combination of growth and multiple expansion over the next few years. Add dividends into the mix, and annualized returns would easily clear my usual ~10% hurdle rate.

Conclusion

As earnings seasons go, this one is going to be particularly well followed, not so much for the results themselves, but for what executives will be saying about the rest of the year. On that basis, Unity Bancorp delivered a pretty upbeat performance, batting away tariff concerns alongside an impressive set of financials. The impact of a nasty downturn would be my main concern here, but a sub-9x P/E does look cheap for the mixture of quality and growth that this bank appears to offer.

data.title: Danger Ahead, Hold These Cash Funds
data.description:
Introduction

My portfolio is about 20% cash currently.

Even though I think recession fears are overblown, I am expecting market volatility to continue until the US settles the trade disputes that have led it to have the highest effective tariff rate in over a century.

That's up from a more typical 2.5-5% position because I believe that we are yet to see the peak of this new volatility regime. I never let cash go below 2.5%, but that's just personal preference.

For the record, we've already seen volatility levels not seen since the COVID-19 pandemic and the Great Financial Crisis before then, this year.

US investors are strapped into the rollercoaster now, with markets selling off and bouncing back violently through this recent volatility spike.

Warning: Danger Ahead

I foresee more volatility ahead, particularly at the end of this month. On April 30th, the US will get its Q1 GDP reading.

Current estimates foresee a deep contraction in the US economy, which is likely to cause shocks to the market. GDPNow, the Fed's tool for forecasting real GDP, is estimating a 2.8% contraction for Q1 2025, that is the quarter that ended March 31st.

There's unfortunately no getting off this rollercoaster, unless you want to go to cash. So, that's exactly what I did.

Solution: Cash

I've been advocating cash essentially since I began writing on Seeking Alpha, and have accumulated a few cash funds of my own that I use to hold my own "dry powder," that is funds I am waiting to deploy into future investments, or cash that I am holding explicitly as an investment (so, not savings and emergency funds).

I hold two funds that make up my cash position:

  1. The iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SGOV)
    1. This fund owns T-Bills, the safest form of US Treasury.
    2. Its FWD yield is 4.2%.
    3. Its yield is derived from the current Federal Funds Rate.
  2. The Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (NYSEARCA:JAAA)
    1. This fund owns AAA CLO tranches, the safest form of CLO debt.
      1. AAA CLO tranches are made up of below-IG debt, with safeguards in place to create less risky instruments from risky investments.
    2. Its FWD yield is 5.25%.
    3. Its yield is tied to short-term rates like T-Bills, but it earns a premium.

During turbulent times, these funds have held their ground (mostly), and have provided significant yields for investors in our current rate environment, which, I believe, will last longer than folks expect, given current economic data.

Note the "mostly" in the paragraph above. What I'm talking about here is that there has been a recent divergence in the performance of SGOV and JAAA, where JAAA has developed (and undeveloped, then re-developed) a discount to its NAV that has caused paper losses to shareholders who needed the liquidity and couldn't wait to sell their shares.

This is not a problem we see in SGOV recently, which has spared it from the same criticism that JAAA has befallen.

That's just the price changes, to show the difference in how these two funds have performed YTD. Here is JAAA's discount outright:

These movements have not caused me to re-evaluate my position on JAAA, and I have been happy with how it has stabilized following this volatility event, where the VIX hit the mid-50s. We can see the overlap between the VIX spike and the discount on JAAA.

Keep this correlation in mind for the future, and invest accordingly. I think this gives us a good onus to follow this guidance:

Both SGOV and JAAA are fine cash positions to have, as instability and volatility events that harm JAAA are temporary. However, cash waiting to be deployed into investments at a moment's notice should be held in SGOV, as it is not as adversely affected during these moments, and is more liquid during high volatility events like market crashes (see you on April 30th).

That doesn't mean to not hold JAAA as a cash position that you want to use as a shield, that is, to collect the 5%+ yield while you wait for general market prices to fall, or for economic conditions to change.

If you are not trying to directly time the market day-to-day, i.e., "being willing to sit out for now," JAAA is still very suitable for your cash position. This is how I am using it, and so the discounts present to me as an opportunity to move cash into the fund cheaply.

Make sure that you are clear on that risk if you are considering JAAA as a part of your cash position.

Conclusion

I am waiting out this market volatility, and have shifted a heavy piece of my portfolio into cash. This cash is deployed across two major funds:

  1. The iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF
  2. The Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF

Both have pros and cons to them, with SGOV being more liquid for those wanting to time markets and deploy cash tactically, but yielding less; JAAA carries a higher yield with similar risk, but can have issues during volatility spikes with its liquidity, making it less ideal for traders and more ideal for those wanting a longer-term cash position.

However, both seem like better investments to me than the markets do, as I foresee future pain ahead, particularly as recession fears are stoked with a (potentially, but heavily expected) negative GDP reading on April 30th. I will re-evaluate my stance following that event, and until then, I am loading up on cash.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Golden Ocean: Strong Buy Today
data.description:
Golden Ocean (NASDAQ:GOGL) is one of the largest public dry-bulk shipping companies in the world. They've been public since 1996 and are listed on both the Oslo and New York stock exchanges.

Historically, I wouldn't have been interested in Golden Ocean - they never seemed to trade as cheaply as other dry bulk shipping companies. One of the reasons for this is that they are traditionally well managed.

However, of late, their share price has dropped significantly. In addition, there is a very strong chance they'll be subject to a takeover bid from Cmb.Tech (CMBT). As a result, I rate Golden Ocean to be a strong buy.

I think that based on the current share price, Golden Ocean is a reasonable purchase. But that the interest from Cmb.Tech makes it exceptionally attractive. Firstly let's look at the company and the share price and then consider the upside scenario from there.

The share price

This is the Golden Ocean share price today. Currently, they're trading at $7.39/share, giving them a market capitalisation of just under $1.5 billion.

Is this cheap? Their 52-week high was $15.77, and their low was $6.27.

So they are trading 17% over their 52-week low, but nearly 60% off of their 52-week-high. So I would suggest that technically they appear cheap. Which is always a good place to start.

It is - of course - worth considering the recent price action in Golden Ocean. It goes without saying that markets have been volatile. The below shows us the S&P500 for 2025 so far. Golden Ocean is down around 20% year to date compared with around 8% for the S&P500. This is a not a mega-cap name, in a relatively volatile sector. This volatility gives you the opportunity to invest in Golden Ocean today, at what I consider to be a good entry price.

The company

Golden Ocean is big. They now own 59 Capesize and 32 Panamax vessels. (Note: they owned 51 Capesize as at 31/12/2024 but since then, they exercised an option for 8 Capesize vessels, to bring them up to 59 Capesize vessels today.)

They pay a very healthy dividend, a total of $1.05 per share in 2024. On that basis, they are yielding 14% (on a trailing 12-month basis). Golden Ocean is a shareholder friendly business.

John Fredriksen owned 41% of the firm (via Hemen Holdings) and clearly exerted significant influence over the company. This partly led to Golden Ocean being one of the better managed listed dry-bulk firms.

Their earnings and their balance sheet

The below is their Profit & Loss from their Q4 2024 (and full year 2024) earnings presentation:

Based on the full-year 2024, Golden Ocean earned $1.12. Which gave them a Price / Earnings ratio of around 7. In and of itself that suggests Golden Ocean is a cheap stock and would be good value for an investment today.

Next let's look at their balance sheet as at the end of 2024. The below is from their 2024 earnings release:

GOGL had cash and short-term assets of $285 million as at 31/12/2024. But they also had significant levels of debt. Their total liabilities are $1.479 billion.

The Enterprise Value for Golden Ocean is the market cap plus liabilities, less their cash (and short-term assets).

That gives us $1.5 billion + $1.479 billion - $285 million = $2.694 billion. If you purchased the company today and repaid all debts, that's the price you would have to pay. That makes it very large compared with most traded shipping companies today.

To get an idea about their cashflows we can look at their P&L again:

Their net income for the full-year 2024 was $223 million. We can add back in depreciation and their interest expense to this number: $223 million + $141 million + $101 million = $465 million. So if you paid $2.694 billion for the company today, you could expect to get your money back and own the fleet after 6 years. Again, that looks like excellent value to me and in itself suggests Golden Ocean is a buy.

Current market conditions

Q1 2025 will be bad for Golden Ocean. They'll achieve only $15k/day for Capes and $10k/day for Panamax. After this, Q2 will see an improvement:

It's worth noting that dry-bulk rates are very seasonal. FFAs show a strong pick-up in Capesize rates for April, Max and the rest of the year:

If 59 Capes earn $20k/day, and 32 Panamax earn $10k/day for a year, Golden Ocean should see revenue of $550 million - $600 million in a year. This would be down on their 2024 revenue of $968 million and would put pressure on their cashflows. However, GOGL should retain some cashflows in this scenario.

This situation would clearly put pressure on the company, and on my thesis that represent good value. And this scenario assumes current market rates. However, I think it's a little unfair:

1. FFAs are just a snapshot. In 2024, rates for Capesize vessels were as high as $35k/day and as low as $11k/day. We cannot rely just on FFA markets, though they are a helpful indicator.

2. if rates remain low, as they are today, companies tend to scrap vessels. In this situation, the supply of vessels reduces and the price to hire a vessel should increase. The best cure for low prices is low prices!

3. the supply/demand scenario. GOGL highlight this in their Q4 slides:

  • In 2025, the gross supply of Capesize vessels is +2%
  • 30% of the Capesize fleet is now over 15 years of age. Older ships are more expensive to run. They require more regular dry-docking and special surveys, putting them out of action for weeks a time, and incurring costs. This effectively reduces the size of the active fleet.

4. The fleet size isn't growing dramatically. But there has been growth in demand:

Clearly if demand increases, and supply is constrained, rates should increase as a result.

5. Golden Ocean can sell ships. Yes GOGL has exercised options to purchase ships in Q1 2025. But these are options and - I assume - were in the money for GOGL. They are simultaneously rumoured to have sold two of their older Panamaxes. Ships are relatively liquid assets, and provide GOGL with a considerable amount of flexibility.

Q1 2025 will not be a good quarter for Golden Ocean. And FFAs would not suggest rates are very profitable for the full year. But these are one quarter, and represent a snapshot of economic conditions. Dry Bulk and shipping is cyclical, and at the moment I still think there is more upside than downside, and that Golden Ocean still has plenty of levers to pull on.

The upside

The reason I'm interested in Golden Ocean today is two-fold.

The first is the share price has dropped significantly. Which makes today a very good entry price. But the second - more interesting reason, is the presence of Cmb.Tech

Original Purchase:

Firstly in March they purchased 41% of Golden Ocean from John Fredriksen at a price of $14.49 per share.

This is no small purchase! They spent just under $1.2 billion just one month ago to make this investment. Today one share costs $7.39...so they have lost 49% on that original investment.

Subsequent Purchase:

But they haven't stopped there. They've since increased their position to 49.4% of the company, adding an additional 8%:

If Cmb.Tech felt that Golden Ocean was good value at $14.49 per share...it must look considerably better value today!

It could be the case that CMBT are looking to take a passive investment in GOGL. However, this is certainly not their history. CMBT itself is a merger between Euronav and CMB, and is led by the Saverys family. I'm not familiar with the entire history here...however it seems very unlikely that the active, acquisitive Saverys family, with a recent history of transactions, would then invest their capital in a passive stake.

Recent Rumours

This is the rumour that surfaced surrounding Golden Ocean and Cmb.Tech:

Which would suggest that CMBT were considering a purchase today at around $8.72 x 1.3 = $11.33. That gives you 53% upside from today's price.

Cmb.Tech 13D

The below is a link to the 13D filing CMBT made after their early purchase of GOGL shares:

0000919574-25-001904

There are a few items I'd call out here:

1.

They are borrowing to buy shares in Golden Ocean. They borrowed $1.15 billion so far. That's about how much they have spent on purchasing shares in Golden Ocean. But in addition, they have agreed an additional $250 million to be used for buying Golden Ocean

I'd note as well that the loan is spread amongst 3 well-regarded institutions.

And the document itself gives some details as to the fact that Cmb.Tech is looking at a merger of the two companies:

And then they detail explicitly as well that Golden Ocean is the target. And that an exchange of shares is likely. This makes the purchase significantly more affordable!

So CMBT have a strong history of purchases, have access to the funds to afford a purchase, have outlined a plan to make the purchase...and the purchase price has come down considerably! I would suggest they are going to complete a buy-out promptly!

Management

One other point which I have considered of late is Golden Ocean's management. They appointed a new CEO (their previous interim CEO & CFO) and a new CFO. I don't consider these moves to be anything except the relatively day-to-day management of the company. These are in-house appointments.

GOGL – Appointment of CEO and CFO – Golden Ocean

Risks

As with any investment, there are certainly risks. Golden Ocean is a shipping company, and as such it is a volatile, cyclical investment. The main risks as I view them:

1. the overall market. If stock markets continue to trade poorly, there is a chance that Golden Ocean trades in line with them. If markets trade down from here, Golden Ocean would suffer as well.

2. the dry-bulk market. This is a cyclical market. The Capesize and Panamax vessels transport grains, iron ore and coals. If the market for these basic products falls, then the demand for GOGL's fleet will fall in tandem and GOGL's earnings would fall.

3. there is one specific risk I need to call out. If it was the case that CMBT decided to sell their holdings here, the stock price itself would come under massive pressure. I clearly view that as a very unlikely scenario, but of course, it could happen.

Summary

I think Golden Ocean is well priced today, if you want exposure to the dry-bulk market.

But I think an investment today is significantly more attractive than that, as I think Cmb.Tech is clearly considering a buy-out of the total company. To my mind, all of the evidence is pointing to Cmb.Tech making a bid to purchase Golden Ocean. As a result, I think you could see considerable upside to the share price from here, in the near future. To my mind, Golden Ocean is a strong buy today.

data.title: AMD Stock Is Now A No Brainer (Technical Analysis, Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Intro

Since my hold rating back in January, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) (TSX:AMD:CA) has seen declines of nearly 20%. I believe the recent selloff has created a no-brainer buy opportunity in the stock. In the below analysis, the technicals are shown to still be mixed, but with the emergence of positive signs, I believe the tide is turning in favour of the bulls. As for the fundamentals, most recent earnings showed strong results. Even better was their guidance, as revenue growth is expected to accelerate while gross margin expands for their Q1. The P/S ratio, on the other hand, has consistently trended down, suggesting irrational pessimism and undervaluation in the stock. As a result, I upgrade AMD to a buy rating, as the outlook for the stock is getting increasingly bright at this point.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

AMD was in a strong long-term downtrend since at least early October. The stock was able to break above in mid-March but plunged back below due to the market rout. However, the stock has been able to recover and is now back above this trend line. Therefore, the downtrend may have ended for AMD. Other than this former downtrend line, there are no other support levels in this timeframe, while there are three significant resistance levels above. The first one is at around 96, just a hair above the current stock price. This level was a key support in early March and seems to have acted as resistance recently. The next source of resistance would be at 101 where an early April downside gap occurred. This level was also a brief ceiling for the stock in early March. Lastly, there is likely to be resistance at about 115 as this area was support in January while being resistance in late February and late March.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has remained below the 200-day SMA for the entirety of the past six months, a signal of sustained long-term bearishness. In addition, the gap between the SMAs is currently at a significant level, and so bearish momentum has been strong as of late. For the near term, the EMAs had a bearish crossover at the end of March to show that the balance of power had shifted to the bears. However, the gap between the 9 day and 21 day EMAs has been closing in the past few days and so near term bearish momentum is actually now receding.

The stock broke above the Bollinger Bands midline in mid-March to show that the long-term downtrend was beginning to weaken. AMD did drop back below, however, and is still currently below the midline despite the near term recovery. A breakout above would be a confirmation signal that the downtrend is indeed over. For the MACD, there was a bearish crossover at the start of this month, but the histogram has turned positive for the past few trading days, signalling that bearish momentum has peaked. In addition, a bullish crossover seems to be imminent as the MACD line continues its rebound. Despite the stock making a new six-month low recently, the MACD made a comparable low to the one in December, and so strength may be holding up for the stock in the longer run. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of 47.12 after being below 30 just recently. This is a major shift in sentiment, as the bears are now only in control by a very modest amount. The bulls have bounced back and the next challenge for the RSI will be to break above the 50 level. The RSI did make a slightly lower low recently than back in December, though. Lastly, the stochastics had a bullish crossover just days ago, with the %K breaking above the %D. Both lines broke above 20 as well to show the receding of bearish sentiment. The %K has levelled out a bit, but both lines may still challenge the 50 level in the near future. Furthermore, the stochastics shows bullish divergence as it made a higher low this month compared to back in late February.

Takeaway

In my view, there's beginning to be promising signs developing in the technicals for AMD. The chart shows that the stock has broken out of its downtrend despite resistance remaining heavy. The SMAs show that long term bearish momentum is considerable, but the EMAs show that the near term is turning in the bulls' favour. Lastly, the indicators show encouraging short-term signals in addition to some early bottoming indications in the MACD and stochastics. While the technicals are overall still mixed at this point, there are strong reasons to believe that the worse is over for AMD.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the early October high as the starting point and the recent low as the end point, key price targets are given in the above chart. The nearest level at 23.6% coincides with the 100 level in the stock. In addition, the early April downside gap occurred very near this level, and so resistance in that area is expected to be considerable. This 23.6% retracement level may have already contributed as resistance on the recent major bullish candlestick. Moving up, the 38.2% level could also be significant. This would be the low to mid-110s in the stock and as you can see, the late February and late March peaks were around this area. Recall that the area at around 115 was identified as a resistance level in the above analysis.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock remains below the cloud but as you can see, the stock's rally has pushed it quite close to the Leading Span A, and so while general bearishness is still present, it may be weakening. The cloud remains bearish as well, but moving into May, there are some promising signs. While the Leading Span A and B have dropped into mid-May, note that the cloud is thinning as the deviation between the two Leading Spans decrease. Therefore, while the cloud's resistance is moving lower, its strength may not be too significant moving forward. The Conversion Line crossed below the Base Line earlier this month to show that momentum had shifted bearish for the near term, but currently, the gap between these lines is remaining stable and so there is no acceleration in downward momentum at this point. The stock has broken above the Conversion Line's resistance, but may be experiencing some difficulty at the Base Line. Lastly, the Lagging Span is now only a hair below the early March stock price. This is another signal that bearish momentum is on the decline and that the bulls are regaining strength. Overall, I believe investors should be encouraged by the Ichimoku Cloud's current state, as there are clear indications that the stock may be headed towards a phase of strength.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

AMD released their FY2024 Q4 earnings back in February and showed robust results in their financials. The company reported revenues of $7.66 billion, up 24% YoY, and an adjusted EPS of $1.09, up 42% YoY. Their all-important Data Center segment saw revenues grow by 69% YoY to a record $3.9 billion as they reported a strong ramp in their Instinct GPU shipments as well as growth in their EPYC CPU sales. Performance versus expectations was slightly mixed as revenue beat estimates by $132.12 million while EPS came in-line with expectations. As you can see in the chart provided above, revenue growth showed QoQ improvement as the rebound in the figure continues. It is now back to above average levels, showing that the business is performing in a strong manner. Another important metric worth mentioning here is their cash flow from operations. They reported $1.30 billion for this figure, representing growth of over 240% YoY. This is by far the highest growth seen in the past few years, and so clearly the business is firing on all cylinders. Lastly, their margins are trending in the right direction as adjusted gross margin increased from 51% to 54% while adjusted operating margin increased from 23% to 26%. This shows the company's efficiency is improving by a healthy amount. Overall, there is really not much to complain about in their Q4 results, as widespread strength is present in these financial metrics.

Guidance

AMD provided brief guidance figures for their FY2025 Q1. They are expecting to see revenues of $7.1 billion +/- $300 million, with the midpoint representing YoY growth of 30%. This would be an acceleration of growth versus their Q4's 24% growth rate, and so the fundamentals of the business are expected to continue strengthening. This implied growth rate would also represent an even higher above average level in the figure than in Q4. They are also projecting an adjusted gross margin of 54% for their Q1, which is a nice improvement from FY2024 Q1's 52% figure. Overall, I would say that the guidance they provided is solid and shows that AMD is likely to experience fundamental strength moving forward.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio has shown major contraction since its early calendar 2024 high, and even YTD contraction has been significant as AMD started 2025 with an around 8.0 reading. The P/S ratio is now currently at a reading of 6.074, a far below average level, and I believe it shows significant undervaluation in the stock. As I discussed earlier, revenue growth has recovered to now an above average level as the QoQ acceleration continues to play out. Furthermore, in their FY2025 Q1 guidance, revenue is projected to increase by 30%, another acceleration in growth. Revenue growth is therefore expected to move even farther above its multiyear average. Guidance also states an increase in gross margins YoY, which should boost the P/S ratio as well. Instead, the multiple has been on a steady downtrend that leads me to believe that there is a major disconnect between the valuation and the financials of the company. While the fundamentals look increasingly strong, the P/S ratio has fallen to far below average levels. The median P/S ratio for the Information Technology sector is only 2.51 right now, but I do believe that this major premium can be justified with the company's strong position in AI datacenter infrastructure, and it's still accelerating growth.

Conclusion

In my previous article, I stated that the time to buy AMD was near. In this article, I'm stating that the time has now come to buy the stock. The technicals were shown to be still mixed from the analysis above but with key positive signs emerging, I believe there are reasons to believe that the bottom is either in or near. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings show that the business is performing at a strong level. Even more encouraging is their impressive guidance that shows growth acceleration and margin expansion. While this is the case, the P/S ratio has actually shown sharp contraction, leading to my undervaluation assessment for the stock. Therefore, I upgrade AMD to a buy rating as the improving technicals and bullish fundamental setup makes the stock a no-brainer.

data.title: Interactive Brokers Q1: Rating Upgrade Despite -8% Drop Post Market
data.description:
Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) reported a mixed Q1. Revenue jumped to $1.43 billion, exceeding analyst estimates by a modest 1.42%, and the adjusted EPS climbed to $1.88, falling short of street expectations by -2.66%. Initially, post-market, the stock saw declines of approximately -3%, but as the conference call started, the stock began to decline further to the point where it is now -8.03% down at 6:26 PM NY time due to potential client deleverage concerns. With this, the shares consolidated a drawdown of -32.2% from all-time highs, compared to the -12% drop of the S&P 500.

In a way, this massive drop could also be driven by increased EPS revisions weeks before the earnings announcement, which didn't materialize as the stock fell short of EPS expectations.

All in all, revenue saw a yearly rise of 19.2% and 0.7% QoQ. Meanwhile, EPS grew 16.8% YoY and fell -7.38% sequentially. At the same time, the company announced two corporate events. First, the quarterly dividend was raised from $0.25 to $0.32, indicating an appealing 28% rise. Second, the firm announced a 4 x 1 stock split that will take effect on the 18th of June.

A key highlight of the quarter was the record new accounts added at 279 thousand, even surpassing the retail trading frenzy of early 2021, when Interactive Brokers added 252 thousand accounts. In total, the brokerage firm consolidated 3.616 million accounts in the quarter, representing a 32% rise YoY and an 8% QoQ. Simultaneously, customers' equity sat at a record $573.5 billion, which was modestly up 1% for the quarter and 23% for the year due to strong market performances.

Commission revenue came in strong, rising 36% YoY and ending at $514 million, driven by a strong 41% rise in executed orders of 725 million. Meanwhile, net interest income saw a modest YoY rise of 3% at $770 million. Despite interest rates dropping, the net interest income remains higher than the past three years, at levels of $747 million in 2024, $637 million in 2023, and $282 million in 2022 due to higher client accounts.

in millions Q1 2025 Q1 2024 Q1 2023 Q1 2022
Net Revenues $1,427 $1,203 $1,056 $645
Non-Interest Expenses $372 $337 $295 $251
Overhead Ratio 26% 28% 28% 39%

Source: IBKR IR

From the expense side, non-interest expenses rose to $372 million, rising 10% YoY but displaying a slightly lower overhead ratio of 26% that declined for the third year in a row. This means that the company was more efficient in managing its non-interest expenses relative to the revenue.

During the quarter, IBKR introduced prediction markets in Canada and for professional clients in the European Economic Area. The company announced during the conference call that they plan to roll out ForecastX contracts to the general EEA client base very soon but didn't give any color on the current volumes of prediction markets. In addition, Interactive Brokers added seven different cryptocurrencies to their portfolio, including Solana, Cardano, Ripple, Chainlink, and more.

During the earnings call, management provided some context about recent activity amid the market turmoil. The two key points were that they are seeing a strong rise in foreign accounts due to the market drop and that they have experienced record volumes due to volatility. Nonetheless, they have seen a market deleverage with fewer clients trading options and a decrease of -12% in margin loans.

"We have seen very significant volumes as the market dropped and then it bounced back up, we saw record volumes. There were some shifts that we noticed our clients traded less fewer options. As far as the deleveraging is concerned, we saw a slight decrease around10% to 12% decrease in margin loans, which is something you would expect when there is such large move downward, you would expect the customers to reduce their risk posture. We also saw somewhat less aggressive positions in options and futures. I think that roughly summarizes what we've recently seen." Milan Galik, CEO

The Drawdown Has Allowed The Valuation To Be Fair

With the post-market price of $159.50 and trailing GAAP EPS of $7.39, the shares of Interactive Brokers now trade at a PE multiple of 21.6x, which is in line with its 5-year average of 21.2x. In February, the valuation hit a peak of 34.0x PE ratio that has substantially dropped -36.5%. In my previous analysis of Interactive Brokers, I rated the shares as a "sell," in part because the valuation diverged significantly from the historical average and because the company was breaking many records, making an exit strategy appealing due to the cyclicality that the financials of brokerage firms could experience.

Takeaway & Stock Rating

Currently, the company is also breaking records, as reported in all key line items of Q1. Nonetheless, I expect this to revert in Q2 due to lower asset values that would affect revenues and lower risk appetite that would reduce leverage activity. Despite the outlook not being too appealing, Interactive Brokers is a well-positioned company exhibiting fast growth through the years, that offers investors the necessary tools to operate every kind of market, both retail and institutional, and has a strong international presence as compared to peers such as Robinhood (HOOD), E*Trade (MS), Charles Schwab (SCHW), but closely aligned with the recent market debutant Webull (BULL).

To conclude, the new valuation of Interactive Brokers is substantially fairer than months ago, and despite a market backdrop that is a headwind to market activity in the mid-term, I will upgrade my previous "sell" rating to a "hold" on the shares of Interactive Brokers.

data.title: Aristotle Global Equity Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
(All MSCI index returns are shown net and in U.S. dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Markets Review

Global equity markets started the year on a modestly negative note, with the MSCI ACWI Index returning -1.32% for the first quarter. In contrast, global fixed income gained ground, as the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index rose 2.64%. Value stocks outpaced growth over the quarter, with the MSCI ACWI Value Index outperforming the MSCI ACWI Growth Index by 11.59%.

Latin America and Europe were the strongest regions, while North America was the only negative performer and Japan increased the least. On a sector basis, eight out of the eleven sectors within the MSCI ACWI Index finished higher, with Energy, Utilities and Financials rising the most. Meanwhile, Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services were the weakest performers.

Inflation trends remained broadly stable across developed markets. The U.S., U.K., and EU all reported annual inflation below 3%, giving central banks greater flexibility. The Federal Reserve held rates steady, while the European Central Bank and Bank of England continued to ease monetary policy in response to softer growth signals. In Asia, China’s economy recorded 5% GDP growth in 2024, showing tentative signs of recovery, while the Bank of Japan raised interest rates for the third time since ending its negative interest rate policy in March 2024 on expectations of sustained 2% inflation.

Trade policy re-emerged as a market concern during the quarter. In his first months back in office, President Trump announced a new wave of tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico and China, with additional warnings directed at the EU and other trading partners over what were termed “imbalanced trade arrangements.” The targeted industries—autos, steel and aluminum—reflected a focus on reshoring and industrial policy. While the long-term impact is still unfolding, the renewed trade uncertainty prompted central banks to lower growth forecasts and adopt a more cautious tone on future rate moves.

In Europe, Germany’s economy remained under pressure after contracting for a second consecutive year in 2024—the first two-year contraction since 2003. In response, Chancellor-designate Friedrich Merz proposed sweeping fiscal reforms, including an overhaul of the constitutional debt brake, a €500 billion infrastructure fund and increased defense spending. While potentially supportive of medium-term growth, these initiatives have raised concerns around inflation and fiscal discipline, adding complexity to the European Central Bank’s policy path.

On the geopolitical front, a fragile ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in January unraveled by March amid disagreements over hostage releases. In Ukraine, a military stalemate in the east opened the door for ceasefire negotiations brokered by the U.S., with discussions reportedly tied to a potential minerals agreement to offset financial aid.

Finally, the artificial intelligence theme continued to influence market narratives. Chinese startup DeepSeek gained international attention after launching a low-cost rival to leading generative AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic and Google. The move not only intensified competition but raised new questions about the durability and configuration of global AI supply chains. Notably, DeepSeek’s approach—achieving comparable performance at a fraction of the cost—has challenged assumptions around the infrastructure demands of generative AI, potentially reducing the need for GPUs, energy-intensive data centers and the broader hardware stack the market had expected would underpin AI growth.

Performance and Attribution Summary

For the first quarter of 2025, Aristotle Capital’s Global Equity Composite posted a total return of 1.12% gross of fees (1.02% net of fees), outperforming the MSCI ACWI Index, which returned -1.32%, and the MSCI World Index, which returned -1.79%. Please refer to the table below for detailed performance.

Performance (%) 1Q25 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception*
